content: Bald eagles have eyesight 4 to 8 times sharper than humans. They can spot a rabbit from 3 kilometres away. Their eyes occupy about half their skull — if human eyes were proportionally as large, each would be the size of a billiard ball. Eagles also see ultraviolet light, which helps them spot fish scales shimmering under water.

Bald eagles are masters of thermal soaring, using rising columns of warm air to gain altitude without flapping. With a wingspan of about 2 metres, they can soar for hours covering hundreds of kilometres with minimal energy. When diving for fish, they fold their wings and plunge at speeds up to 120 kilometres per hour.

By 1963, only 417 nesting pairs of bald eagles remained in the continental United States. The pesticide DDT was poisoning their fish prey, causing eggshell thinning so severe that eggs crushed before hatching. After DDT was banned in 1972 and nesting sites were protected, populations recovered dramatically to over 300,000 individuals today.

Bald eagles build the largest nests of any North American bird. A typical nest is 1.5-2 metres wide and 1 metre deep, but some reach 3 metres wide and weigh over 2 tonnes. Eagles mate for life and return to the same nest year after year, adding new material each season. Both parents take turns incubating 1-3 eggs for about 35 days.

Bald eagles primarily eat fish, which they snatch from the water surface with their powerful talons. They also eat waterfowl, small mammals, and carrion. Their talons can exert a grip force of about 400 pounds per square inch. Eagles are also skilled kleptoparasites — they often steal fish from ospreys and other birds.

The bald eagle has been the national bird and symbol of the United States since 1782. Benjamin Franklin famously argued against the choice, preferring the wild turkey. The bald eagle appears on the Great Seal, presidential flag, and many government insignias. The name 'bald' comes from 'piebald', meaning white-headed, not hairless.

Some bald eagle populations migrate seasonally, traveling thousands of kilometres between breeding and wintering grounds. Eagles in northern regions fly south to areas with open water for fishing. They can fly at altitudes up to 3,000 metres during migration and cover over 200 kilometres in a single day.
